query: most common cause of post neonatal mortality

retrieved_docs.title: Fever in a Neonate -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: WHO statistics cite over one million neonatal deaths around the world each year result from neonatal sepsis/pneumonia making it the leading cause of infant mortality. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the estimated incidence of early-onset neonatal sepsis in the United States is 0.77 to 1 per 1000 live births. With the establishment of guidelines for universal screening and treatment of maternal GBS colonization, the incidence of early-onset sepsis in full-term infants has decreased to 0.3 to 0.4/1000 live births. However, the incidence of late-onset sepsis remained the same (0.4/1000). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal MIS-C -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Short-term outcomes of MIS-C depend on the severity and extent of cardiovascular and respiratory involvement. [25] Various studies have reported mortality ranging from 1 to 11%. [26] [27] [28] With timely diagnosis and management, outcomes are favorable. Long-term follow-up in infants exposed to COVID-19 antenatally or in the neonatal period is necessary to elucidate potential sequelae.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hyperglycemia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Increased mortality risk among preterm and VLBW infants

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Sepsis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality rates are inversely proportional to gestational age, such that preterm or younger neonates have higher mortality rates than term neonates [22] . E. coli has also been found to be associated with a higher mortality rate when compared with GBS. As noted above, the introduction of GBS intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis has decreased mortality rates caused by GBS. The treatment of clinically suspected neonates with negative cultures has also significantly decreased mortality rates. Preterm infants with sepsis may develop impaired neurodevelopment. Also, others may have vision impairment. Those infants pretreated with aminoglycosides may also develop ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Birth Asphyxia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Birth asphyxia is associated with high morbidity and mortality. Long-term complications can range from mild to life-threatening. The condition is reported to have a mortality of over 30%, with the majority of deaths occurring within the first few days after birth. Those infants who survive are often left with mild-to-severe neurological deficits, and they also may end up dying from aspiration or systemic infections. Long-term survivors have been found to have disabling cerebral palsy, inadequate mental development or low psychomotor scores, seizures, blindness, and severe hearing impairment. [3] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the decrease in mortality, neonatal meningitis continues to have high morbidity. Global mortality estimates are approximately 190,000 cases per year. [50] In Western countries, the mortality rate is about 10 to 15%, with the highest rates in preterm neonates. [2] In low-income countries, the mortality rate is as high as 58%, with moderate to severe neurodevelopmental impairment in approximately 23% of survivors. [51] In high-income countries, the rate of neurological sequelae is 20 to 50%. [50] [52] A meta-analysis of neurodevelopmental outcomes in children with GBS meningitis noted that 32% had neurodevelopmental impairment at 18 months, including 18% with moderate to severe impairment. [53] Furthermore, compared to the general population, the risk of death remains higher five years after the acute illness. [50] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Birth Asphyxia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of term perinatal asphyxia is 2 per 1000 births in high-resource countries. The rate is 10 times higher in countries with limited maternal and neonatal care access. [7] Of those infants affected, 15% to 20% die in the neonatal period, and up to 25% of survivors are left with permanent neurologic deficits. [8] In preterm infants, the cause of perinatal asphyxia is often unclear, and its incidence can be particularly high, especially in low-resource settings. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A study from Tunisia revealed a neurologic complication rate of 21.6%. Respiratory distress, low birth weight, shock, and pleocytosis of fewer than 500 cells/mm3 were indicators of a worse prognosis. Adding ofloxacin to the antibiotic regimen was associated with decreased neurological sequelae in survivors. [55] Another study noted that infant feeding difficulties and concomitant pneumonia were prognostic predictors of poor outcomes. [56] Also, high CSF protein, both during and after acute illness, has been linked to poorer outcomes. [56] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric and Neonatal Resuscitation -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: While older age often correlates with improved outcomes, postarrest hypoxic-ischemic brain injury continues to contribute substantially to morbidity and mortality in pediatric patients. Continuous EEG monitoring is recommended following ROSC in high-risk or comatose patients to detect subclinical seizures. Anticonvulsants are indicated only when seizures are observed. Autopsy with potential genetic testing should be strongly considered in cases of unexplained death.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm and Term Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PPROM and PROM) -- Complications -- Maternal Risks
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal complications associated with PROM include: Intrauterine infection Sepsis Placental abruption Antenatal, intrapartum, and postpartum hemorrhage Death

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Care of the New Mother -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: During the first week of the postnatal period, severe hypertension, severe bleeding, and infection are the most common contributors to maternal deaths, while cardiovascular cause is the leading cause of late deaths. [27] Compared to developed countries such as Norway and New Zealand, the US has significantly lagged in providing adequate prenatal care. US mortality and morbidity are significantly higher ( 17.4 % vs. 1.7 % ), and the US has a significantly lower number of maternal healthcare providers, such as obstetricians and midwives ( 19 vs. 65 per 1000 live births ). Earlier postpartum visits are mandatory to evaluate for resolution of postpartum blues and other chronic medical conditions such as hypertension and diabetes and to improve both maternal and neonatal mortality and morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm and Term Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (PPROM and PROM) -- Complications -- Fetal and Neonatal Risks
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal and neonatal complications associated with PROM include: Neonatal sepsis Complications of prematurity Respiratory distress syndrome Intraventricular hemorrhage Necrotizing enterocolitis Retinopathy of prematurity Hypothermia Glucose and electrolyte abnormalities Failure to thrive Death Complications of prolonged oligohydramnios Pulmonary hypoplasia Limb contractures and skeletal deformities [1] [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the incidence of SIDS in 2017 was 35.4 per 100,000 live births in the United States. Prior to the introduction of campaigns to reduce SIDS deaths, death rates were noted to vary significantly between Asian populations, aboriginal people in Australia, the population of the United Kingdom, the population of the United States, and the population of New Zealand. Death rates varied from as low as 0.3 deaths per 1000 live births (among Asian populations in the US and Bangladeshi's in the UK) to 7.4 deaths per 100 live births (in the Maori people in New Zealand). Cultural norms, child-rearing practices, and socioeconomic factors appear to explain the variation. [2] In the United States, CDC reports reflect similar variations among non-Hispanic Black Americans, Native Americans, the non-Hispanic white population, Hispanics, and the Asian population. [3] [4] The peak incidence occurs between 2 and 4 months, and 90 percent of cases occur before six months of age. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, Black women are twice as likely as White women to experience prolonged cardiac dysfunction; when recovery occurs, it takes twice as long. Mortality rates can reach 20%, with the highest rates observed among Black women in the United States and women in less developed regions. The condition can profoundly impact affected individuals, their families, and newborns during a critical life stage.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Complications -- Maternal Complications
retrieved_docs.content: SPTB has been associated with modest cardiovascular mortality and morbidity risks that remain elevated more than 40 years later. [66] The reason for this association remains unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Infections -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of antenatal infections can significantly impact both maternal and fetal health, leading to increased morbidity and mortality. Understanding these potential outcomes is vital for timely intervention and the prevention of long-term adverse events. Maternal complications include the following: Sepsis, postpartum hemorrhage, uterine rupture, and disseminated intravascular coagulation Increased risk of preterm labor and delivery Fetal complications include the following: Premature birth, low birth weight, and developmental delays Congenital effects (eg, microcephaly, congenital heart defects, vision or hearing loss in CMV, Zika) Neonatal infection and mortality in severe cases

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical incompetence (قصور عنق الرحم) is classified across multiple terminologies as a disorder of the female internal genitalia that weakens the cervical canal, often presenting with a patulous cervical os and abnormal uterine cervix morphology. It is a child of “Female internal genitalia structure” and belongs to “Other specified female genital disorders” and the broader group “Abnormality of organs and soft tissues of pelvis complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.” Clinically, cervical incompetence places the patient in a high‑risk pregnancy category and is strongly associated with preterm delivery, low birth weight in infants (INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT), neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة), fetal death (موت الجنين), post‑partum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), and other complications of birth and the puerperium. It often co‑occurs with related obstetric conditions such as polyhydramnios and other amniotic cavity problems, which in turn are linked to pleural
